g CURRERT AFFAIRBS,

The Great Northern Railway has published a list of

* thirty successful candidates at its first competitive
examination. By the way, the //rceman and Arnott’s
" Limes ought to be grateful to us for this new source of
advertising revenue! Judging by the names it would
seem that of the thirty successful candidates twenty are
““TIdolators.” Notwithstanding  the marks for

s ““ manner ”’ the ‘‘ Idolators’’ have done very well, but
it may be that the authorities did not care to utilize
their ‘‘ manner, etec.,”’ veto power at the first examina-
_ tion. This railway has been convicted by our revela-
tions of a considerable amount of bigotry in the staffing
~ of their offices, and we have no confidence whatever in
. bheir impartiality, and if the marks for “manner,”’ etc.,
re not utilized-heavily for bigot purpose this time, they
e used on future occasions.

We work it out that the average number of marks for
““manner,”’ etc., given to the ten saved’’ was 445,
whilst the average for the twenty ‘‘Idolators'' was
340. An average difference as against the ‘“ Idolators’
of 105. In the ‘“ manner, ctc.,”’” part of the scheme of
oxamination the candidates were before the inquisitors,
and the names and religion of the candidates were
known. When we return to the other part of the exami-
nation, the real literary competitive examination, in
which the examiners knew nothing of the candidates, we
find that the average number of marks for the ten
‘“saved ”’ was 947, and the average of the twenty non-
‘“saved ’’ was 923—a difference as against the ‘‘ Idola-
tors ”’ of 24 : the difference against the ‘‘ Idolators *’ in
the other case was 105!

Lever’s firm, that are the ringleaders in the British
Soap Combine, into which one Irish firm unfortunately
have been drawn, issue a periodical called Progress. It
is published ‘ specially as a means of inter-communica-
tion between the Head Office and works at Port Sunlight,
the branch offices in the United Kingdom, and the
offices, agencies, oil mills and associated companies
abroad.””  We have a copy of the November issue of
this soap paper. We are sure our Irish readers will be
complimented on the fact that a real British soap paper
condescends to notice this country, and that their feel-
ings of gratitude will develop into love when they have
read the following paragraph:—

“Phwat? Thry th* Susuicur Soap, is ut? Lave me alone wid
yer SUNLIGHT S0AP.  SUNLIGHT S0aP, indade, an’ phwat for shud
Oi thry it, Oi'd like to know? Git me that MULDOON'S MOIGHTY
Wasuer Oi ordhered, an’ don’t play anny pranks on me, young
man. Div ye think Oi'm wan of them giddy crithurs as'll thry
ivvry new-fanglet priparation as comes along? Phwat's that? It's
bin out this twinty years? Has ut, indade? Oi don’t care if ut's bin
out twinty toinwes twinty years, Give me MULDOON'S, begorrah, and
none other. It’s a foine soap, an’ nawthin’ comes up to ut, so lave
me alone wid yer SUNLICHT contrapshuns, or yer moonlight, for that
matther I”

We take it that this was written by the dirty suds
cditor, and evidently he thinks that indulgence of
ignorant British buffoonery is calculated to popularise
the British soap in Ireland, for under the heading
‘“ Correspondence > we read the following further
graceful reference to this savage country: —

Arrah, Bridget, acushla, it’s soory wo are we cannot make you
any “allowance” for your kind promise of loyalty to SunLiGHT
SoAr.  You state in your lotter that ““ you are greatly pleased with
SuNLIGHT S0AP,” and you couldn’t have said anything more calcu-
lated to gratify us. But it's a mighly “quare” proposition you
make. Indeed it is. You ask: “Clan you make any allowance it
I usc it as long as I live?’ And you, as you say, only two years
married, and but 2L years old. That's a long time to contract
for, isn’t it, Bridget? It speaks volumes, however, for your
confidenco that Su ur will never bo boaten. Under the circum-
stances, don’t"you think, Mrs, Bridget McA. , that the “Soap
is its own roward ?”’

The ranting anti-Trish, anti-Catholic bigot, Mr.
Campbell, K.C., has been holaing forth at a ‘‘saved
literary society at Leeson Park, Dublin. It is interest-
ing to note that this anti-Irish person is one of the chief
counsel engaged by Mr. William O’Brien, M.P., in his
action against an advertiser of the  Spring Chicken,”
the Freeman’s Journal. This particular counsel for the
ex-William of the People said:—

Thero were, he took it, in this country four and a half millions
of o population, and of that number there were about a million:
and a half of Protestants of all denominations, who had come to
the conclusion, with fow oxceptions—tho exceptions might almost .
bo well worthy of being preserved in a museum—that their civil
and religious liberties depended upon tho maintonance of the
Union, that the only guaranteo they have for their civil and reli-

- gious rights is to bo found in the fact that no laws could govern

thom excopt such as had always received the sanction of the
Imperial Parlisment. They delighted to bo considered a portion

178 . ;

of the Empire of Groat Britain. Thoy rejoiced in its good for-
tuneg, and sympathised in its sorrows, and their sons were serving
the interests of the Empiro in every part of the habitablo globo.
In that connection they had the solo and only guaranteo for their
civil and religious rights.

We fear that Mr. John Sweetman, one of Mr. Edward
Martyn’s principal licutenants, is incorrigible. ~ We
see that he has actually recognised a Zoyal Commission
by giving evidence before it. Now Edward VIL.
ordered the Commission of Inquiry into the Depart-
ment, and as Edward VII. has had no authority in
Ireland during the last, or present, century owing to
the Renunciation Act of 1783—at least that is what
Mr. Edward Martyn says, and he ought to know—Mr.
John Sweetman had no right as a loyal follower of Mr.
Martyn to give evidence before a Commission whose
establishment was distinctly wltra vires—was in fact a
piece of impertinence to Mr. Edward Martyn’s party
on the part of Edward VII. And yet Mr. John Sweet-
man c9udones it, nay, by an act of his own volition,
recognises this Royal Commission as legal.

There has been no law in this country since the re-
vocation or squashing, or whatever it was, of the Re-
nunciation Act of 1783—according to Mr. Edward
Ma}'tyn. The law courts are figments of a Parliamen-
tarian imagination and have no moral force; the police-
men that moved on Skeffy are, in the constitutional
order, even more airy things than astral bodies, yet Mr.
Edward Martyn is appealing to this law, though law died
with the upsetting of the Renunciation Act of 1783,
Mr. Martyn is appealing to this base immoral law with
a view to getting redress for having been sent out of
the landlords’ rendezvous, the Kildare Street Club, a
place from which common sense people might have ex-
pected Mr. Martyn would have voluntarily departed
when he ceased to be a Unionist.

Writing to us under date 29th October, Messrs W.
Dennehy and Sons, the well-known Irish hat manufac-
turers of Cork, say :— You will, we know, be glad to
hear that we have been declared contractors to the Post
Office for supplies of headgear for Great Britain and
Ireland. We need hardly say that the competition is
of the very severest, and the contract has never, we
believe, gone outside London.  This is only the thin
edge of the wedge, and we mean to annex all the
Government and other contracts possible. We are glad
to say that our progress is becoming more and more
marked. Of course, a bran-new industry (as far as
machinery processes are concerned ours is new) has a
lot of up-hill work. New hands have to bo trained, and
they take a bit of time to digest. However, we have
our trade on a sound commercial basis, and its develop-
ment is only a question of time. We cannot close with-
oub expressing our appreciation of your journal as an
advertising medium.”’

We see that the Naas Union recently accepted a con-
tract forBritish-mado blacking. How is it that they
passed over the firm of Malone Brothers!

As the amount of matter in Irish to bc_ta,ught in the
schools this year is, wo ‘understand, being 'much dis-
cussed by managers and teachers, the following, which
is written by one who ought to know what he is talking
about, will be read with particular {xltew‘est by both
managers and teachers :—Irish in National Schools.—
Extra subjects being mno longer recognised by the
National Board, provision should now be made to
include Trish in the ordinary cwrriculum of schools in
which it has been hitherto regarded as an extra, branch.
Where Trish is being introduced for the first time into
the junior standards some modification of the official
programme should be applied for by the teacher. It is
pointed out in the Notes to the Official Programme that
the cowrses for the different standards are graduated on
the assumption that the pupils have learned the courses
prescribed for the preceding standards, and that where
vhis has not been done the pupils may take the course
suited to their knowledge of the subject. A grouping gf
standards is also permitted. Bearing these hints in
mind, the following would appear to be a suitable amount
of work for teachers to submit for approval to the
Board as a programme in Irish for their schools for this
year.  Similar programmes have already been sanc-
tioned : —Trish as Ordinary Subject—Programme for
1906-7.—Infants and first standard : “ Ceachta Beaga,”’
Part 1. (Miss Borthwick). First eight lessons. Second
and third standards: ¢ Ceachta Beaga,”” Part I. (all).
TFourth and fifth standards, and any pupils who have
never learned Irish before: “ Ceachta Beaga,” Parts I.
and TI. To write an Irish copy and repeat twenty lines
of poetry. Where Irish has been taught and paid for
as an extra, the following will apply—Pupils who have
passed first yoar extra course: “ Ceachta Beaga,’”’ Parts
I., II. and ITI. Irish writing and forty lines of poetry.
Those who have passed second year extra course “Cathair
Conroi,”” or an alternative book as in official programme
for fifth standard. Where there are pupils remaining
at school who have passed in third year’s Irish it would
be well to vary the texts used in the upper standards
from year to year. Irish should be used as far as pos-
sible in addressing the pupils, more especially during
the Irish lesson. “Cainnt”’ colour cards, price 4d..

0’Donoghue’s “ Mion-Chainnt Scoile,” 2d. Feargus
Finnbheil’s ¢ MacLeighinn,” 6d., etc., will be
found useful by teachers to give oral drill.  Where

there is only one teacher available to teach Irish to all
standards it will be better for him to work on the bipar-
tite system instead of attempting to teach it to all the
§chool together. TFor instance, let him take the seniors
in the morning, say, from 10 to 10.30, whilst juniors are
in the desks writing, etc., and give the juniors their
less(_)n later‘on in the day whilst seniors are writing.
Senior pupils might be utilised occasionally to teach
Irish to the juniors.—Finghin na Leamhna.

Combine on Monday night. Mr. Thomas Byrne stated
that a great many traders would not give the Dublin

firm that had joined the Combine any orders so long
as thoy were mombers of the Trust. If Mr. Barrington
has not been able to say that he sold an extra ounce
of soap as the result of Industrial Revival he may
find as a result of his joining this British Combine
that in the future he will sell an ounce or two loss.
We are very sorry, indeed, that Barringlon's went over
to the British Trust, and would be glad to hear that
they thought better of it and threw in their lob with
the other Trish independent manufacturers. The worst of
Barrington’s going over to the English Trust is that they
aro very large makers of toilet soaps, and there is, there-
fore, a difliculty for tho present anyway before those
consuniers who desire to support Irish manufacture and
are equally determined to cold-shoulder all connected
with the British Trust.  We are glad to be able to
state, however, that Messts. Dixon & Co., an Irish and
independent, firm that have nothing to do with the
British Trust, have decided to make what is called
Milled Toilet Soaps, and they are now looking after
the necessary machinery. We congratulate Messrs.
Dixon & Co., of the Erne Works, Dublin, on their
promptitude and- enterprise in meeting the situation
created by the British Trust, and we think we can
safely say that their Milled Toilet Soaps will get a
hearty welcome to the Irish independent soap market
from our readers. In the meantime any other indepen-
dent Trish firm that have milled toilet soaps to sell
ought to let the Irish consumer know about them.

The National Theatre Society have arranged a triple
bill for Saturday night. Like the circuses in country
places the performances will be for one night only, and
we would impress this fact on the minds of our readers,
as sometimes the Theatre indulges in a week’s run.
Mr. Synge’s one-act {ragedy, ‘‘ Riders to the Sea,”” a
play about which various opinions are held, will lead off
the triple bill. Tady Gregory’s translation of Moliere’s
comedy, the title of which she translates as ‘‘ The
Doctor in Spite of Himself,”” will be produced from
directions kindly supplied to the Abbey Theatre by the
Secretaire of the Comedie Frangaise. The last item will
be Lady Gregory’s very amusing ‘‘ Hyacint Halvey,”
about which we wrote a notice on its first appearance.

We thought that Kinsale had been by this time some-
what affected by the Irish movement. There was a
‘“ grand ’’ concert there on November 1st, at the Tem-
perance ITall, and on the programme we do not find as
much as one Irish song. Evidently the Kinsale tem-
perance party like to take sentiment hot with their
spring water, for we fin. songs with such sweetly melting
titles as “ When in the twilight,”” ‘‘ Come where the
moonbeams linger,”” and ‘In the dusk of twilight.”
We often hear of what the Irish-Ireland movement has
done for Temperance; we would like to hear a little
more of what the Temperance party are doing for Irish-
Ireland. What a contrast between this un-Irish con-
cert at Kinsale and the entertainment we recently drew
attention to that took place at the Temperance Hall at
Scariff in Co. Clare.

Flute Band.”” There is no mention of a drum even.
Well, the ceremony of unfurling the banner of these
musical ‘‘ Faith Defenders '’ of Ballymoney, where the

M.P., the 1_'zmting Orange deadhead. One Mr. J.
Young was in the chair on the momentuous occasion,
when the bannor of the ‘“ Faith Defenders’ Protestant
TFlute Band ”’ was unfurled. The chairman said that
Mr. Moore was one of the most brilliant men that
Ballymoney had ever produced! Was the chairman
talking sarcastic like? Ballymoney is a village of less
than threo thousand inhabitants. The compliment
paid to Mr. Moore, ex-M.P., reminds us of the remark
of a literary reviewer who once wrote that a certain
volume of verse was the greatest epic—of the week !

The London dona¢ or rather the sona¢ in London,
was a great success. The attendance was really good;
during the first and second days, 2 constant stream of
poople visited the exhibition, and on the third day the
hall was packed with men and women from all parts of
London, and the hall is one of the very largest in the
town. TIts kindly and genial atmosphere was the chief
charm of the Aonach, and the three Irish plays and
the wholly Irish concerts were enthusiastically received
by everyone present. By the way, on Monday next,
the President of the Gaelic League, Dr. Douglas Hyde,
will address a public meeting in London, and we trust
that a large muster of Irish in London, whether mem-
bers of the Gaelic League or not, will assemble at eight
o’clock, at the Bishopsgate Institute, Bishopsgate St.,
without.

A new departure of the Irish Art Companions has a
smack of enterprise about it. During the past year
their show-room at 27 Clare St., Dublin, has been
used as a sale depot for many of the smaller Irisn
industries; and their experience in this direction has
suggested the establishment of a permanent exhibition
of Trish goods. It is now proposed to add to the exist-
ing show-room a large exhibition hall adjoining it
which, till recently, was occupied by the industrial
department of the Companions, which has been lately
transferred to a larger factory at Ringsend. The in-
creased accommodation will also include two large rooms
on the first floor in the Clare St. premises. The pro-
moters intend to show in this permanent exhibition
samples of Irish industries , or where this is not feasible,
as in the case of perishable goods, etc., illustrated
placards with the names and addresses of the manufac-
turers and retail warehouses in which the goods are
stocked. We note that Dr. Windle, Dr. Hyde, and
Mr. R. A. Anderson will act as a vigilance committee
on behalf of the exhibitors to see that the financial pro-
visions set forth are strictly adhered to. <The concern
will be under the business management of Mr. P. J.
Gleeson, to whom applications for space may bg sent
to the offices, 28 Clare St.

Recently a largely attended meeting in connection
with the Central Branch (Belfast) of the Catholic De-
fence Society was held in the Catholic Boys’ Hall.
Councillor Magee presided, and Mr. Savage, P.L.G.;
Dr. N. J. McDonnell, J.P.; Mr. J. L. McDonnell, and
Mr. J. Coleman addressed the meeting, and it was
decided to establish a committee of the Catholic Defence
Society in the Fall’s District. ~ We are, curiously
enough, indebted to a report in the Orange Belfast
Bvening Telegraph for this information. A report was
sont to the ‘truly national’ and ‘truly (‘J:\Lhol‘lc”
local daily, the Zrish News, but it was refused insertion !

by no means unlikely—it is difficult to estimate the

% Now, in so far as the inevitable manhood suffrage is
in this direction an evil, there is only one cure for it,
and that is womanhood suffrage. ~We have advanced
so far on the logical path of universal suffrage, and the
property qualification at present oxacted has become so
much a theoretical and vexatious sham, that the full
demand must soon be conceded. If you let in those
who are at present let in, you can give no satisfactory
reason why you shut out those who are at present shut
out. The logical course adopted by the rest of the
world must one day triumph. But if it is logical and
just to extend the franchise to all men, even the most
degraded and miserable, it is but a corollary that it
should be extended to women. If the citizen voter
shall have to produce no credentials save the proof of
his existence, a woman has those credentials as well as
a man. But this is just the logical conclusion which the
French Government shrinks from drawing.  With its
present policy, it dare not admit women to the franchise.

Universal sufirage then will work well if it be true
universal suffrage. The impoverished masses may some-
times be inclined to rush to extremes. ITeaven knows,
they have not a little to urge them to it. But the sobriety,
virtue and piety of women will always be an antitode.
I do not say that women will not in religious matters
be capable of exercising a will of their own.  Most
women I have ever met have had very definite and
sensible opinions on such topics. But as long as
religion has any hold on a country at all, the women
will never consent to its being unfairly treated, much
less persecuted. The true glory of a government is not
in its camps or its princes or its great men. The record
of its achievements is to be found in the home. It is
there that the forces of good and evil tell, and woman
reads that record day by day. She judges not by the
blatant generalities of the Press, but by the all-impor-
tant little things of life. To her religion is not an
““ism.”” She thinks of the drunken husband or the
dutiful child, of the ever-present consolation in mis-
fortune. She will no more be turned against the Church
than she will be brought to favour the publican. If
there is any cause-that should call for the whole-hearted
support of a thinking Irish priest it is the extension of
the franchise to women.

name, as denominational almost as we could desire.
In most of its schools there is no mixed education
whatsoever. Tt is separate education, as it ought to
be, for the children of different religious professions,
and phus it has come, in a great part of Ireland, to be
a help rather than a hindrance to the Church. That
is a great achievement. It has not been the work of a
day, but it has been brought about by the steady and
unswerving determination of a Catholic people who
were true to themselves and loyal to their pastors.’
And yet this is one of the systems of education which,
according to “ Jacques,”” p. 6, “is in such a bad state
that no tinkering with details can do any good.””  True,
a little later on, contradicting himself, he says that it
needs only to be “improved.” But, as we shall see,
in. addition to withdrawing from the Bishops
the right of appeal by the Catholic teachers against the
clerical managers, though it has been working effec-
tually—so another direction in which he wishes it to
be improved is to change, in the first instance, the
status of the managers and lay the foundation for
their being #mproved out of existence. But more of
‘this later on. The Bishops of Ireland, who know some-
thing of what they are writing about, and to whom the
education of their flocks is one of their deepest con-

substance, have pointed out, in their Pastoral, some of

gible; and the maintenance of the model schools, which
violate the principle of local control and managership,
which is the very essence of the whole system, is a
. wrong ‘to Catholics and an unpardonable waste of
public money.””

Since that Pastoral was issued, the position of the

teachers, their salaries, pensions, arbitrary dismissals
by the Board, etc., have occupied the public attention,
and over and over again, by voice, pen and resolutions,
have the Bishops of Ireland called for fair trcatment
for this wonthy body of public servants. It may be
no harm to add that the real friends of the national
school teachers, instead of calling for a “revolution
| in the primary system of education, as was done the
3 other day by a Catholic layman at Belfast, ignorant
! probably of the meaning and effect of his words, will
| work on lines in which they can have the assistance of
il their truest and most powerful friends, the episcopacy
and priesthood of Ireland. Their grievances can be
redressed without a revolution or radical reformation
of the system. It is a question of equitable financial
treatment by a Treasury that has been for years robbing
them and the nation at large of educational grants
which are their dues. If they press forward this point
unitedly and determinedly, brushing aside other issues,
which are being raised, not by their friends, but by
their enemies, the teachers of Ireland are sure to have
their wrongs righted. “The reform of the National
Board,”” says His Grace, the Archbishop of Cashel, in
his letter to the meeting held at Tipperary, on the
20th of the month, “so as to make it representative of
our educational interests, and sympathetic in point of
administration, deserves the support of everyone in-
terested in the success of primary education; but any
radical change, likely to interfere with the management
of our schools, by transferring them to local commit-
tees, can never secure the approval of the Hierarchy of
Ireland.” And in his letter to the Limerick meeting,
held on the 13th, at Limerick, the Bishop of Limerick,
whilst expressing his sympathy with the movement for
the improvement of the financial position of the
teachers, adds, “but I feel bound to say that some of
the sweeping and revolutionary changes which have
i been proposed recently for a remedy, go far beyond the
necessities of the case. We might as well ask a man to
pull down his house, because it wanted a few slates.
Your grievances will be redressed. Public opinion will
not, tolerate the preent starvation of education in Ire-
land, which is as bad under the present as the late
Government, to continue much longer.”

This may seem a digression; but the extracts are
valuable at the present moment, and have an important

need of a “radical change’”? The Bishops do not say
